title: Definition of Crypto-Asset
content: Under Regulation (EU) 2023/1114, a crypto-asset is defined as a digital representation of a value or of a right that is able to be transferred and stored electronically, using distributed ledger technology or similar technology. This definition is intentionally broad and technology-neutral, covering any digital asset that relies on cryptographic validation and distributed ledger infrastructure regardless of the specific consensus mechanism or blockchain protocol employed. The definition excludes financial instruments as defined under MiFID II (Directive 2014/65/EU), deposits as defined under the Deposit Guarantee Schemes Directive, funds except where they qualify as e-money tokens, securitisation positions under the Securitisation Regulation, and insurance or pension products. This broad definition is critical because it establishes the regulatory perimeter for the entire MiCA framework: any digital asset meeting this definition that is not otherwise regulated under existing EU financial services legislation falls within MiCA's scope.

title: Definition of Asset-Referenced Token (ART)
content: An asset-referenced token (ART) is defined under MiCA Article 3(1)(6) as a type of crypto-asset that is not an electronic money token and that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing another value or right or a combination thereof, including one or more official currencies. ARTs are distinguished from e-money tokens by the fact that they reference a basket of assets, commodities, or currencies rather than a single fiat currency. This category encompasses stablecoins backed by multiple currencies, commodity-backed tokens, and tokens stabilised through algorithmic mechanisms referencing external values. The definition is deliberately broad to capture the wide range of stabilisation mechanisms that exist in the market. Issuers of ARTs face heightened regulatory requirements under MiCA Articles 16 through 47, including authorisation requirements, reserve asset management obligations, and ongoing supervisory oversight, reflecting the potential systemic risk these tokens pose to financial stability and monetary policy transmission.

title: Definition of Electronic Money Token (EMT)
content: An electronic money token (EMT) is defined under MiCA Article 3(1)(7) as a type of crypto-asset that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing the value of one official currency. EMTs are functionally equivalent to electronic money as defined in Directive 2009/110/EC but are issued on a distributed ledger. The key distinguishing feature of an EMT compared to an ART is that an EMT references exactly one single official currency (e.g. EUR, USD), whereas an ART may reference a basket of assets or currencies. EMT issuers must be authorised as credit institutions under CRD IV or as electronic money institutions under the E-Money Directive. EMTs must be issued at par value with the referenced currency and must be redeemable at any time at par. Holders of EMTs have a claim against the issuer denominated in the referenced currency. The regulatory framework for EMTs under MiCA Articles 48 through 58 builds upon and supplements existing electronic money regulation.

title: Definition of Utility Token
content: A utility token is defined under MiCA Article 3(1)(9) as a type of crypto-asset that is only intended to provide access to a good or a service supplied by its issuer. Utility tokens are distinguished from other crypto-assets by their functional purpose: they grant the holder access to a specific product, service, or platform rather than representing a financial investment or a stable store of value. Despite their non-financial primary purpose, utility tokens remain within MiCA's regulatory scope and are subject to the general requirements applicable to crypto-assets other than ARTs and EMTs. Issuers of utility tokens must still prepare and publish a crypto-asset white paper and comply with marketing communications requirements. However, they benefit from a lighter regulatory regime compared to ART and EMT issuers. The classification of a token as a utility token requires careful analysis of its actual functionality and use, not merely the label applied by its issuer, as regulators will look through marketing descriptions to assess true economic function.

title: Definition of Crypto-Asset Service Provider (CASP)
content: A crypto-asset service provider (CASP) is defined under MiCA Article 3(1)(15) as a legal person or other undertaking whose occupation or business is the provision of one or more crypto-asset services to clients on a professional basis. Crypto-asset services are enumerated in Article 3(1)(16) and include custody and administration of crypto-assets on behalf of clients, operation of a trading platform for crypto-assets, exchange of crypto-assets for funds or other crypto-assets, execution of orders for crypto-assets on behalf of clients, placing of crypto-assets, reception and transmission of orders for crypto-assets on behalf of clients, providing advice on crypto-assets, providing portfolio management on crypto-assets, and providing transfer services for crypto-assets on behalf of clients. CASPs must obtain authorisation from the competent authority of their home Member State before commencing operations. The CASP framework under MiCA Articles 59 through 76 establishes comprehensive conduct-of-business rules, organisational requirements, and prudential standards that mirror, in adapted form, those applicable to investment firms under MiFID II.

title: Obligation to Publish a Crypto-Asset White Paper
content: Article 4 of MiCA establishes the fundamental requirement that no person shall make an offer to the public of a crypto-asset other than an ART or EMT, or seek the admission of such a crypto-asset to trading on a trading platform, unless that person is a legal entity, has drawn up a crypto-asset white paper in accordance with Article 6, has notified the white paper to the competent authority in accordance with Article 8, and has published the white paper in accordance with Article 9. The white paper must be published on the issuer's website on a date no later than the starting date of the offer to the public or the date of admission to trading. The obligation applies to all crypto-asset issuers within the EU regardless of the size of the offering. Failure to comply with the white paper requirement constitutes a regulatory infringement subject to administrative penalties and measures under Articles 111 through 113. This requirement ensures investor protection through mandatory disclosure before any public offering.

title: Content of the Crypto-Asset White Paper
content: Article 6 of MiCA prescribes the mandatory content of the crypto-asset white paper for crypto-assets other than ARTs and EMTs. The white paper must contain information about the issuer or the person seeking admission to trading, information about the crypto-asset project, information about the offer to the public or admission to trading, information about the crypto-asset and any rights and obligations attached to it, information on the underlying technology and protocols used, and information on the risks relating to the crypto-asset, the issuer, and the project. The white paper must contain a clear and unambiguous statement that the crypto-asset may lose its value in part or in full, that it may not always be transferable, and that it may not be liquid. The white paper must also include a summary which shall be concise, written in plain non-technical language, and shall provide key information about the offer to help prospective holders make an informed decision. The white paper shall be fair, clear, and not misleading, and shall not contain any material omissions.

title: Marketing Communications for Crypto-Assets
content: Article 7 of MiCA establishes requirements for marketing communications relating to an offer to the public of a crypto-asset or admission to trading. All marketing communications must be clearly identifiable as such, and the information in marketing communications must be fair, clear, and not misleading. Marketing communications must be consistent with the information in the crypto-asset white paper. Marketing communications must include a clear and unambiguous statement that a crypto-asset white paper has been published and indicate the address of the website where the white paper is available, along with the contact details of the issuer. No marketing communication may be disseminated prior to the publication of the crypto-asset white paper, and this requirement is without prejudice to the ability of the offeror to conduct market soundings. The competent authority of the home Member State may require the modification or cessation of any marketing communication that is misleading or inconsistent with the white paper. These provisions protect prospective holders from misleading promotional materials and ensure that marketing aligns with formal disclosure documents.

title: Publication of the Crypto-Asset White Paper
content: Article 9 of MiCA specifies the publication requirements for crypto-asset white papers. The white paper must be published on the issuer's website in a machine-readable format on a date no later than the starting date of the offer to the public or the admission to trading. The white paper shall remain available on the issuer's website for as long as the crypto-asset is held by the public. Published crypto-asset white papers and any modifications thereto must be transmitted to the competent authority of the home Member State. The competent authority shall make available the white paper through the register established under Article 109 without undue delay and no later than the starting date of the offer or admission to trading. The white paper must be available in at least one official language of the home Member State or in a language customary in the sphere of international finance. If the crypto-asset is offered in other Member States, the white paper must be available in an official language of each host Member State or in English. These provisions ensure broad accessibility and regulatory transparency.

title: Right of Withdrawal for Retail Holders
content: Article 10 of MiCA grants retail holders of crypto-assets other than ARTs and EMTs a right of withdrawal from the purchase agreement. Where crypto-assets are offered directly by the issuer or by a third party acting on the issuer's behalf, retail holders who purchase those crypto-assets directly from the issuer or third party shall have a right to withdraw their agreement to purchase those crypto-assets within 14 calendar days. The right of withdrawal is exercisable without giving any reason and without incurring any cost. The issuer must inform prospective holders about the right of withdrawal in the crypto-asset white paper. The right of withdrawal does not apply where the crypto-asset is admitted to trading on a trading platform prior to the purchase, since in this case the price is determined by market conditions and the holder can sell on the secondary market. The issuer must refund the amount paid by the retail holder without undue delay and in any event within 14 days from receipt of the withdrawal notice. This consumer protection measure mirrors similar rights under EU distance selling regulations.

title: Obligations of Issuers of Crypto-Assets (Ongoing)
content: Article 12 of MiCA sets out ongoing obligations for issuers of crypto-assets other than ARTs and EMTs. Issuers must act honestly, fairly, and professionally in the best interests of holders of crypto-assets. Issuers must maintain a website that provides information to holders about the issuer, the crypto-asset, and any crypto-asset services provided. This information must be fair, clear, and not misleading. Issuers must have effective arrangements in place for the prompt handling and proper investigation of complaints received from holders and must communicate the procedures for the handling of complaints free of charge. If an issuer discontinues its activities, it must adopt a plan for the orderly wind-down of those activities. The issuer must inform holders of any material change that may affect the value or rights attached to the crypto-asset. Issuers are liable to holders for any damage caused by infringement of this Regulation. These ongoing obligations create a continuing duty of care and transparency toward crypto-asset holders.

title: White Paper Exemptions
content: Article 14 of MiCA provides exemptions from the requirement to publish a crypto-asset white paper in certain circumstances. The white paper requirement does not apply where the crypto-asset is offered for free, where the crypto-asset is automatically created as a reward for the maintenance of the distributed ledger or the validation of transactions (i.e. mining or staking rewards), where the offer concerns a utility token providing access to a good or service that already exists or is already in operation, where the crypto-asset holder has the right to use the token only in exchange for goods and services in a limited network of merchants with contractual arrangements with the issuer. The white paper is also not required where the total consideration of the offer does not exceed EUR 1,000,000 over a 12-month period, or where the offer is addressed solely to qualified investors and the crypto-asset can only be held by such qualified investors. These exemptions are designed to reduce regulatory burden for smaller offerings and tokens with limited financial risk profiles.

title: Authorisation to Offer ARTs to the Public
content: Article 16 of MiCA establishes that no person shall offer asset-referenced tokens to the public within the Union or seek admission of such tokens to trading on a trading platform unless the issuer is a legal entity or undertaking established in the Union and has been authorised by the competent authority of its home Member State. The application for authorisation must be submitted to the competent authority and must include specific information about the applicant and the proposed ART issuance. The authorisation requirement does not apply where the outstanding value of the ART does not exceed EUR 5,000,000 over a 12-month period and the issuer is not linked to a network of other exempt issuers, or where the offer is addressed solely to qualified investors and the ART can only be held by such qualified investors. Credit institutions authorised under Directive 2013/36/EU (CRD IV) are not required to obtain separate authorisation under MiCA for ART issuance but must comply with notification and white paper requirements. This provision ensures that ART issuers are established, authorised, and supervised within the EU.

title: Application for Authorisation as an ART Issuer
content: Article 17 of MiCA specifies the information that must be included in an application for authorisation to issue asset-referenced tokens. The application must include the registered office address and LEI of the applicant, the articles of association, a programme of operations setting out the business model, a legal opinion that the ART does not qualify as a financial instrument, deposit, or securitisation position, a detailed description of the governance arrangements, evidence of good repute and appropriate knowledge and experience of the management body, the identity of natural persons who hold qualifying holdings, a description of the internal control mechanisms and risk management procedures, a description of the complaint-handling procedures, a description of the ICT systems and security arrangements, and a description of the procedure for the segregation of reserve assets. The competent authority must assess the application within 25 working days of receipt for completeness and within 60 working days of receipt of a complete application for a decision on authorisation. If additional information is required, the assessment period is suspended until the information is received.

title: ART White Paper Content Requirements
content: Article 19 of MiCA specifies the content requirements for a crypto-asset white paper for asset-referenced tokens, which are significantly more detailed than those for ordinary crypto-assets. The ART white paper must contain information about the issuer including its governance structure, information about the asset-referenced token and the stabilisation mechanism, information about the reserve of assets including the composition and custody arrangements, information about the rights of holders including any redemption rights, detailed description of the technology used, a comprehensive risk assessment including risks relating to the reserve assets and the stabilisation mechanism, and information about the environmental impact of the consensus mechanism. The white paper must contain a clear and prominent warning that the ART may lose its value in part or in full, that it may not always be transferable, that it may not be liquid, and that it is not covered by investor compensation schemes or deposit guarantee schemes. The issuer must keep the white paper up to date and notify the competent authority of any material change.

title: Ongoing Obligations of ART Issuers
content: Article 21 of MiCA establishes comprehensive ongoing obligations for issuers of asset-referenced tokens. ART issuers must act honestly, fairly, and professionally and in the best interests of holders. They must maintain a website providing clear, accurate, and up-to-date information about the ART, the reserve of assets, the rights of holders, and the complaint-handling procedures. ART issuers must establish and maintain effective systems, procedures, and arrangements to monitor, evaluate, manage, and report risks to which they are or may be exposed. The management body of the issuer must be of good repute and possess adequate knowledge, skills, and experience. ART issuers must employ personnel with the knowledge, skills, and expertise necessary for the performance of the responsibilities allocated to them. Issuers must have a business continuity policy and must implement internal control mechanisms including administrative and accounting procedures, risk management frameworks, and information reporting arrangements. These ongoing obligations ensure continuous prudential soundness and appropriate governance of ART issuers.

title: Own Funds Requirements for ART Issuers
content: Article 22 of MiCA establishes own funds requirements for issuers of asset-referenced tokens to ensure they maintain adequate financial resources. ART issuers must at all times have own funds equal to an amount at least equal to the higher of EUR 350,000 or 2% of the average amount of the reserve assets. Own funds must consist of Common Equity Tier 1 items as specified in Articles 26 to 30 of Regulation (EU) No 575/2013 (CRR). Competent authorities may require an ART issuer to hold an amount of own funds that is up to 20% higher than the amount resulting from the standard calculation, taking into account the risk assessment, the quality and volatility of the reserve assets, the volume and value of transactions, and other relevant factors. For significant ART issuers, the minimum own funds requirement is increased to 3% of the average reserve asset amount. These capital requirements ensure that ART issuers maintain sufficient loss-absorption capacity and provide a financial cushion against operational and reserve asset risks.

title: Reserve of Assets for ARTs
content: Article 36 of MiCA requires issuers of asset-referenced tokens to constitute and maintain a reserve of assets at all times. The reserve of assets must be composed and managed in such a way that the risks associated with the assets referenced by the ART are covered and that the liquidity risks associated with the permanent redemption rights of the holders are addressed. The reserve assets must be legally and operationally segregated from the issuer's own estate and from the reserve of assets of other token issuances. The reserve must be managed in a prudent manner ensuring that the value of the reserve is at all times at least equal to the value of the claims of holders. The composition of the reserve must reflect the assets or currencies referenced by the ART. Reserve assets must be placed in custody with credit institutions or authorised crypto-asset service providers, and the issuer must establish an adequate reserve asset custody policy that avoids excessive concentration. Investment of reserve assets is restricted to highly liquid financial instruments with minimal market and credit risk, ensuring the safety of the reserve at all times.

title: Custody of ART Reserve Assets
content: Article 37 of MiCA establishes detailed requirements for the custody of reserve assets backing asset-referenced tokens. Issuers of ARTs must establish, maintain, and implement custody policies, procedures, and contractual arrangements that ensure that the reserve assets are held in custody at all times. Reserve assets must be entrusted to credit institutions authorised under CRD IV or to crypto-asset service providers authorised for the custody and administration of crypto-assets on behalf of clients. The issuer must exercise due skill, care, and diligence in the selection, appointment, and review of custodians. The custodian must ensure that the reserve assets are not used for the custodian's own account and that the reserve assets are protected from claims of the custodian's creditors. The issuer must not appoint the same entity as custodian for more than a percentage determined by the competent authority. The custody policy must be disclosed to the competent authority and must describe the selection criteria for custodians, the monitoring procedures, and the procedures for ensuring segregation of the reserve assets from the assets of the custodians and from other issuers' reserve assets.

title: Redemption Rights for ART Holders
content: Article 39 of MiCA establishes the redemption rights framework for holders of asset-referenced tokens. Holders of ARTs must have a right of redemption at any time against the issuer and in respect of the assets referenced by the ART or, where the issuer is unable to provide those assets, a claim in the monetary value of those assets. The white paper must clearly describe the conditions, including fees, and the procedure for redemption. The issuer must establish and maintain a policy on redemption that sets out the conditions, time periods, and procedures. Redemption may be subject to a fee, but such fees must be proportionate to the actual costs incurred by the issuer. Where the issuer does not offer direct redemption rights to all holders, the issuer must ensure that there are arrangements with third parties to provide liquidity. The issuer must process redemption requests within a reasonable time and no later than the time period specified in the white paper. These provisions are crucial for maintaining confidence in the stability and usability of ARTs and distinguishing them from non-redeemable speculative crypto-assets.

title: Significant Asset-Referenced Tokens
content: Article 43 of MiCA defines the criteria for classifying an asset-referenced token as significant, which triggers enhanced regulatory requirements. An ART is classified as significant where at least three of the following criteria are met: the size of the customer base of the issuer and its shareholders or third parties ensuring the stabilisation function exceeds 10 million persons; the value of the ART issued or, where applicable, its market capitalisation exceeds EUR 5 billion; the number and value of transactions in that ART exceed 2.5 million transactions and EUR 500 million per day respectively; the significance of the cross-border activities of the issuer, including the number of Member States where the ART is used; the interconnectedness with the financial system. Significant ART issuers face enhanced requirements including higher own funds requirements of 3% of reserve assets, more stringent liquidity management requirements, interoperability requirements, and direct supervision by the European Banking Authority (EBA) rather than national competent authorities. The classification as significant is reviewed at least every two years.

title: Authorisation to Issue E-Money Tokens
content: Article 48 of MiCA provides that no person shall offer e-money tokens to the public within the Union or seek admission of such tokens to trading unless the issuer is authorised as a credit institution under Directive 2013/36/EU (CRD IV) or as an electronic money institution under Directive 2009/110/EC (EMD2). This dual authorisation pathway reflects the functional equivalence between EMTs and traditional electronic money. EMT issuers must draw up a crypto-asset white paper in accordance with Article 51 and notify it to the competent authority. The authorisation as a credit institution or electronic money institution is a prerequisite; there is no separate MiCA authorisation procedure for EMT issuance, unlike for ART issuance. However, EMT issuers must comply with the additional EMT-specific requirements set out in MiCA Articles 48 through 58. The white paper notification must occur at least 20 working days before the date of publication. Competent authorities do not approve EMT white papers but may require modifications if they find non-compliance with MiCA requirements.

title: EMT Issuance and Redemption at Par Value
content: Article 49 of MiCA establishes the fundamental requirement that e-money tokens must be issued at par value with the referenced official currency and must be redeemable at par value at any time and at the request of the holder. Holders of EMTs have a claim against the issuer of the EMT denominated in the official currency referenced by the EMT. The issuer must redeem the EMT by paying the holder in funds other than electronic money the monetary value equivalent of the EMT in the referenced currency. The issuer must prominently state the conditions of redemption, including any fees, in the crypto-asset white paper. Redemption may not be subject to a fee except in cases where the redemption request is excessive or unreasonable. No interest or any other benefit related to the length of time during which a holder holds EMTs may be granted by the issuer or by any third party. This prohibition on interest payments is particularly significant as it distinguishes EMTs from deposit-like products and prevents EMTs from being used as savings instruments, maintaining consistency with the characterisation of electronic money under EU law.

title: EMT Reserve Asset Requirements
content: Article 54 of MiCA establishes the requirements for EMT issuers to safeguard the funds received in exchange for e-money tokens. EMT issuers must invest the funds in secure, low-risk assets denominated in the same official currency referenced by the EMT. Reserve assets must be segregated from the issuer's own assets and held with custodians. At least 30% of the funds must at all times be deposited in separate accounts at credit institutions. The remaining funds must be invested in secure, low-risk assets in accordance with the investment guidelines established for electronic money institutions under Directive 2009/110/EC and any applicable regulatory technical standards. EMT issuers must have adequate policies in place for managing the reserve, including stress testing requirements. The safeguarding requirements ensure that EMT issuers maintain sufficient liquid assets to honour redemption requests at all times. For significant EMTs, the requirement to deposit at credit institutions increases to at least 60% of the referenced amount, reflecting the heightened systemic importance and the need for greater liquidity assurance for widely-used EMTs.

title: Significant E-Money Tokens
content: Article 56 of MiCA defines the criteria and consequences of an e-money token being classified as significant, mirroring the framework for significant ARTs. The EBA shall classify an EMT as significant where at least three of the following criteria are met: the customer base exceeds 10 million persons, the value issued exceeds EUR 5 billion, the number and value of daily transactions exceed 2.5 million and EUR 500 million respectively, the EMT has significant cross-border activity, or the EMT is significantly interconnected with the financial system. Additionally, the European Commission, EBA, or ECB may determine that an EMT is significant based on its potential impact on financial stability, monetary policy transmission, or the smooth operation of payment systems. Significant EMT issuers are subject to enhanced requirements including higher deposit thresholds for reserve assets, enhanced liquidity management, and a recovery plan. Supervision of significant EMT issuers is shared between the EBA and national competent authorities. EMT issuers may also voluntarily request significant classification.

title: CASP Authorisation Requirement
content: Article 59 of MiCA establishes that crypto-asset service providers must obtain authorisation from the competent authority of their home Member State before commencing the provision of crypto-asset services. The CASP must be a legal person or other undertaking that has been authorised in accordance with Article 63 or a credit institution, central securities depository, investment firm, or other authorised financial institution that has complied with notification requirements under Article 60. The CASP must have its registered office in a Member State where it carries out at least part of its crypto-asset services. No third-country firm may provide crypto-asset services within the Union unless it does so through a CASP authorised under MiCA — there is no equivalence regime or third-country passport. Authorisation as a CASP is valid for the entire Union through the passporting mechanism set out in Article 65, allowing a CASP authorised in one Member State to provide services across all Member States either through freedom of establishment or freedom to provide services, following notification to relevant host Member State authorities.

title: CASP Own Funds and Prudential Requirements
content: Article 62 of MiCA establishes prudential safeguards for crypto-asset service providers including minimum own funds requirements. CASPs must maintain own funds at all times of at least the higher of the applicable minimum capital requirement or one quarter of the fixed overheads of the preceding year. The minimum permanent capital requirements vary by service type: EUR 50,000 for CASPs providing only advice, order reception and transmission, or placing services; EUR 125,000 for CASPs providing custody, exchange, execution, or transfer services; EUR 150,000 for CASPs operating a trading platform. CASPs must also hold a professional indemnity insurance or a comparable guarantee covering the territories in which services are provided. The competent authority may require a CASP to hold own funds up to two times higher than the calculated minimum based on a risk assessment. These prudential requirements ensure that CASPs have sufficient financial resources to meet their obligations to clients, absorb operational losses, and support an orderly wind-down if necessary. Own funds must consist of CET1 instruments and retained earnings.

title: CASP Authorisation Application Requirements
content: Article 63 of MiCA specifies the comprehensive information that must accompany an application for authorisation as a crypto-asset service provider. The application must include the name and LEI of the applicant, articles of association, a programme of operations setting out the types of crypto-asset services the applicant intends to provide, evidence that the members of the management body are of good repute and possess appropriate knowledge and experience, identity and shareholding of shareholders or members with qualifying holdings, evidence of compliance with own funds requirements, a description of internal control mechanisms and risk management procedures, a description of ICT systems and security arrangements compliant with Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 (DORA), a description of the procedure for the segregation of client crypto-assets and funds, a description of the complaint-handling procedures, and, where applicable, a description of the outsourcing arrangements. The competent authority must acknowledge receipt within 5 working days and assess completeness within 25 working days. The authorisation decision must be made within 40 working days of receiving a complete application.

title: Cross-Border Provision of Crypto-Asset Services (Passporting)
content: Article 65 of MiCA establishes the passporting mechanism for crypto-asset service providers to operate across EU Member States. A CASP authorised in its home Member State may provide crypto-asset services across the entire Union either through the freedom to provide services or through the establishment of a branch in another Member State. To exercise the passport, the CASP must notify the competent authority of its home Member State specifying the Member States in which it intends to provide services, the crypto-asset services it intends to provide, and, where applicable, whether it intends to provide those services through a branch. The home competent authority must communicate the notification to the host competent authorities within 10 working days. The CASP may commence provision of services in the host Member State from the date of receipt of notification by the host authority or at the latest 15 working days after submission to the home authority. No additional authorisation or administrative requirement may be imposed by host Member States. This single-licence framework promotes the single market for crypto-asset services while ensuring supervisory coordination.

title: CASP General Obligations
content: Article 66 of MiCA establishes the general conduct-of-business obligations applicable to all crypto-asset service providers. CASPs must act honestly, fairly, and professionally in the best interests of their clients. They must provide their clients with fair, clear, and not misleading information, including in marketing communications, and must warn clients about the risks associated with purchasing crypto-assets. CASPs must not deliberately or negligently mislead clients about the real or perceived advantages of any crypto-asset. CASPs must make their pricing policies publicly available and must establish and implement policies to identify, prevent, manage, and disclose conflicts of interest. CASPs are required to have adequate arrangements for business continuity and to maintain systems and procedures that are adequate to ensure the security, integrity, and confidentiality of information received. All crypto-asset services must be provided in the same language as the crypto-asset white paper or in the official languages of the Member States in which services are provided. CASPs must comply with applicable requirements under Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 (DORA) regarding digital operational resilience.

title: Custody and Administration of Crypto-Assets
content: Article 67 of MiCA establishes specific requirements for CASPs providing custody and administration services for crypto-assets on behalf of clients. CASPs providing these services must enter into a custody agreement with their clients specifying the terms and conditions, including the custody policy. The custody policy must describe the procedure for safeguarding and the liability of the CASP. CASPs providing custody must segregate client crypto-assets and must ensure that the client crypto-assets are operationally held in a separate manner from the CASP's own crypto-assets. The CASP must maintain a register of positions opened in the name of each client corresponding to each client's rights over the crypto-assets. The CASP is liable to clients for any loss of crypto-assets as a result of a malfunction or hack attributable to the CASP, up to the market value of the crypto-asset lost. CASPs providing custody must implement an adequate policy for the return of clients' crypto-assets or the means of access to such crypto-assets in the event of insolvency. This provision is critical for institutional adoption as it establishes a clear framework for digital asset safekeeping.

title: Operation of a Trading Platform for Crypto-Assets
content: Article 68 of MiCA sets out the requirements for CASPs operating a trading platform for crypto-assets. Trading platform operators must establish transparent and non-discriminatory commercial rules governing access to the platform, including criteria for the admission of crypto-assets to trading. Before admitting a crypto-asset to trading, the platform operator must ensure that the crypto-asset complies with the platform's operating rules and must assess whether the crypto-asset has been the subject of a published white paper. The platform operator must establish clear rules on the types of orders and the procedures for making transactions and must ensure that the price of crypto-assets published on the platform reflects supply and demand. Trading platform operators must make completed transactions public including price, volume, and time of execution. They must maintain effective systems to detect and prevent market abuse and must immediately report any suspicion of market abuse to the competent authority. The platform operator must have systems in place to ensure fair and orderly trading, including mechanisms to manage volatility and prevent flash crashes.

title: Exchange of Crypto-Assets for Funds or Other Crypto-Assets
content: Article 70 of MiCA establishes the requirements for CASPs providing exchange services for crypto-assets. When exchanging crypto-assets for funds or for other crypto-assets, the CASP must establish a non-discriminatory commercial policy specifying the types of crypto-assets it is willing to exchange, the pricing methodology, and any applicable fees. The CASP must publish a firm quote or, alternatively, the method for determining the price at which the exchange will be executed, prior to the execution of the exchange. When executing an exchange, the CASP must ensure that clients obtain the best possible result taking into account the quoted price, costs, speed, and likelihood of execution. After executing an exchange, the CASP must provide a confirmation to the client including the details of the crypto-assets exchanged, the quantity, the total price and any fees. CASPs providing exchange services must maintain records of all exchange orders received and executed for at least five years. This service category covers both crypto-to-fiat exchanges and crypto-to-crypto exchanges and is typically the most commonly used crypto-asset service by retail clients.

title: Providing Advice on Crypto-Assets
content: Article 73 of MiCA establishes the requirements for CASPs providing advice on crypto-assets. CASPs providing advice must assess whether the crypto-asset services or crypto-assets are appropriate for the client based on the client's knowledge, experience, financial situation, and objectives. The assessment must consider the client's understanding of the risks involved, the type and nature of the crypto-assets, and any other relevant information. CASPs must warn clients where crypto-asset services or crypto-assets are deemed not appropriate for the client or where the client does not provide sufficient information for the assessment. CASPs providing advice must have adequate policies and procedures in place to ensure that individuals giving advice possess the necessary knowledge and competence. When providing advice, CASPs must specify whether it is provided on an independent basis, and if so, must assess a sufficient range of crypto-assets available on the market and must not accept fees, commissions, or non-monetary benefits from third parties. These provisions parallel the suitability and appropriateness requirements under MiFID II, adapted for the crypto-asset context.

title: Transfer Services for Crypto-Assets
content: Article 76 of MiCA establishes the requirements for CASPs providing transfer services for crypto-assets on behalf of clients. Transfer services involve the transfer of crypto-assets from one distributed ledger address or account to another on behalf of a client. CASPs providing transfer services must enter into an agreement with the client specifying the terms and conditions under which the service will be provided. The agreement must describe the procedure for the transfer, including the applicable fees and the time required to complete the transfer. CASPs providing transfer services must ensure that the transfer is executed promptly and that the client is informed when the transfer has been completed. The CASP must implement adequate procedures to detect and prevent errors in the transfer of crypto-assets, including procedures to verify the accuracy of the destination address. Where a transfer fails or is executed incorrectly due to an error attributable to the CASP, the CASP must restore the crypto-assets to the originator without undue delay. CASPs must maintain records of all transfers for at least five years and make such records available to competent authorities upon request.

title: AML/CFT Requirements and Transfer of Funds Regulation
content: Article 79 and the broader MiCA framework integrate anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing requirements by reference to existing EU AML legislation. CASPs are classified as obliged entities under the EU Anti-Money Laundering Directives (AMLD4/AMLD5/AMLD6) and must implement comprehensive AML/CFT programs including customer due diligence (CDD), transaction monitoring, suspicious transaction reporting, and record-keeping obligations. Additionally, the Transfer of Funds Regulation recast (Regulation (EU) 2023/1113), adopted alongside MiCA, extends the travel rule to crypto-asset transfers, requiring CASPs to collect, verify, and transmit originator and beneficiary information with every crypto-asset transfer regardless of amount. This represents a stricter approach than FATF Recommendation 16, which applies thresholds. CASPs must implement risk-based AML/CFT policies, appoint a compliance officer, conduct ongoing monitoring of business relationships, and file suspicious activity reports with the relevant Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU). Failure to comply with AML/CFT requirements constitutes grounds for withdrawal of CASP authorisation.

title: Cooperation Between Competent Authorities on AML/CFT
content: Article 81 of MiCA establishes cooperation mechanisms between competent authorities to ensure effective enforcement of AML/CFT requirements. Competent authorities responsible for the authorisation and supervision of CASPs must cooperate closely with authorities responsible for the supervision of compliance with AML/CFT obligations. Where a competent authority discovers facts that could be relevant to the functions of another authority, including AML/CFT supervisors and FIUs, it must communicate those facts without undue delay. Competent authorities may exchange confidential information with AML/CFT supervisors and FIUs to the extent necessary for the performance of their respective functions. Where a competent authority suspects that AML/CFT obligations are being breached, it must notify the competent AML/CFT supervisor and, where applicable, the FIU. The EBA plays a coordination role in facilitating cooperation between national competent authorities and in developing common approaches to AML/CFT supervision of CASPs. This supervisory cooperation framework ensures that AML/CFT risks identified in the context of prudential supervision are effectively communicated to AML/CFT authorities for enforcement action.

title: Prohibition of Insider Dealing in Crypto-Assets
content: Article 88 of MiCA prohibits insider dealing in crypto-assets that are admitted to trading on a trading platform. Inside information is defined as information of a precise nature, which has not been made public, relating directly or indirectly to one or more issuers or crypto-assets, and which, if it were made public, would be likely to have a significant effect on the prices of those crypto-assets. The prohibition covers the use of inside information to acquire or dispose of crypto-assets, the recommendation or inducement of another person to acquire or dispose of crypto-assets on the basis of inside information, and the unlawful disclosure of inside information. The prohibition applies to any person who possesses inside information by virtue of being a member of the management body of the issuer or CASP, by having a holding in the capital of the issuer, by having access to the information through the exercise of an employment, profession, or duties, or through criminal activities. Any person who possesses inside information must not disclose that information to any other person except in the normal course of employment, profession, or duties.

title: Insider Disclosure Obligations
content: Article 89 of MiCA establishes disclosure obligations regarding inside information for issuers and persons seeking admission of crypto-assets to trading. Issuers of crypto-assets admitted to trading on a trading platform must inform the public as soon as possible of inside information which directly concerns the issuer in a manner that enables fast access and complete, correct, and timely assessment of the information by the public. The issuer must post and maintain on its website for a period of at least five years all inside information it is required to disclose publicly. The issuer must not combine the disclosure of inside information with the marketing of its activities. Issuers may delay disclosure of inside information where immediate disclosure would prejudice the legitimate interests of the issuer, delay is not likely to mislead the public, and the issuer is able to ensure the confidentiality of that information. Where disclosure has been delayed, the issuer must notify the competent authority immediately after the information is disclosed and provide a written explanation of how the conditions for delay were met. These provisions mirror the market abuse framework under Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (MAR).

title: Prohibition of Market Manipulation in Crypto-Assets
content: Article 91 of MiCA prohibits market manipulation in respect of crypto-assets admitted to trading on a trading platform. Market manipulation includes entering into a transaction, placing an order to trade, or any other behaviour which gives, or is likely to give, false or misleading signals as to the supply of, demand for, or price of a crypto-asset, or which secures or is likely to secure the price of a crypto-asset at an abnormal or artificial level. Market manipulation also includes entering into a transaction, placing an order to trade, or any other activity or behaviour which affects or is likely to affect the price of a crypto-asset and which employs a fictitious device or any other form of deception or contrivance. Disseminating information through the media, including the internet, or by any other means, which gives or is likely to give false or misleading signals, constitutes market manipulation where the person who made the dissemination derives an advantage or profit from the dissemination. This includes the practice of wash trading, spoofing, layering, and pump-and-dump schemes applied to crypto-assets. CASPs operating trading platforms must establish effective mechanisms to detect and report suspected market manipulation.

title: Supervisory Powers of Competent Authorities
content: Article 93 of MiCA confers extensive supervisory powers on competent authorities for the enforcement of the Regulation. Competent authorities have the power to require any person to provide information and documents, to suspend or prohibit an offer to the public or admission to trading, to suspend or prohibit the provision of crypto-asset services, to require the cessation of any practice contrary to MiCA, and to carry out on-site inspections. They may require the temporary cessation of any practice that the competent authority considers contrary to MiCA and may take all necessary measures to ensure that the public is correctly informed, including by requiring the issuer or CASP to issue a corrective statement. Competent authorities may request the freezing or sequestration of assets, impose temporary prohibitions on the exercise of professional activities, and require existing telecommunications operators to provide data traffic records. They may request that a court or tribunal order the suspension of marketing or the offer to the public. These powers are exercisable directly by competent authorities, in collaboration with other authorities, or by application to the competent judicial authorities.

title: Administrative Penalties and Measures
content: Article 111 of MiCA establishes the framework for administrative penalties and measures applicable to infringements of the Regulation. Member States must ensure that competent authorities have the power to impose at least the following administrative penalties for infringements: a public statement indicating the natural or legal person responsible and the nature of the infringement; an order requiring the natural or legal person to cease the infringing conduct; maximum administrative fines of at least EUR 5,000,000 for natural persons; for legal persons, maximum administrative fines of at least EUR 15,000,000 or up to 12.5% of the total annual turnover, whichever is higher; and maximum administrative fines of at least twice the amount of the benefit derived from the infringement where that benefit can be determined. For infringements of the ART and EMT provisions by legal persons, the maximum fine is at least EUR 5,000,000 or 3% of total annual turnover. Member States may provide for additional types of penalties or for higher levels of fines. Criminal penalties may be imposed where national law so provides. When determining the type and level of penalty, competent authorities must consider the gravity and duration of the infringement, degree of responsibility, financial strength, and cooperation with authorities.

title: Withdrawal of CASP Authorisation
content: Article 95 of MiCA specifies the grounds and procedure for withdrawal of CASP authorisation. The competent authority may withdraw the authorisation of a CASP where the CASP has not made use of the authorisation within 12 months of the date of authorisation, has expressly renounced the authorisation, has not provided crypto-asset services for a period of nine consecutive months, has obtained the authorisation through irregular means including false statements, no longer meets the conditions under which authorisation was granted, has seriously and systematically infringed the provisions of MiCA, falls within a case for which national law provides for withdrawal, or has seriously and systematically infringed national provisions adopted pursuant to the AMLD governing AML/CFT obligations. Before withdrawing an authorisation, the competent authority may restrict or suspend the authorisation or impose conditions. The competent authority must notify the CASP of the decision, which must be reasoned and subject to appeal rights. The decision to withdraw must be communicated to ESMA for removal from the register. The withdrawal of authorisation triggers obligations to protect clients' crypto-assets and ensure orderly wind-down.

title: Client Asset Segregation and Protection
content: Article 75 of MiCA establishes requirements for the protection of clients' crypto-assets and funds held by CASPs. CASPs holding clients' crypto-assets or funds must make adequate arrangements to safeguard the ownership rights of clients, especially in the event of the CASP's insolvency. CASPs must not use clients' crypto-assets for their own account without the client's prior express consent and must ensure that any such use is on terms agreed with the client. Where a CASP holds clients' funds, whether in fiat currency or crypto-assets received in exchange for the crypto-assets to be purchased, those funds must be deposited with a credit institution or a central bank by the end of the next business day. CASPs must ensure that clients' crypto-assets held in custody are identifiable separately from the CASP's own assets and from other clients' assets. CASPs must maintain updated books and records that enable the competent authority to distinguish at any time funds and crypto-assets held for one client from those held for other clients and from the CASP's own assets. These segregation requirements provide critical insolvency protection for clients.

title: Definition of Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)
content: Distributed ledger technology (DLT) is defined under MiCA Article 3(1)(1) as a technology that enables the operation and use of distributed ledgers. A distributed ledger in turn is defined under Article 3(1)(2) as an information repository that holds records of transactions and that is shared across, and synchronised between, a set of DLT network nodes using a consensus mechanism. This technology-neutral definition encompasses blockchain-based systems and other forms of distributed ledger including directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), hashgraph, and other consensus-based data structures. The definition is intentionally broad to avoid becoming technologically obsolete and to capture future innovations in distributed ledger architectures. The significance of this definition within MiCA is that it forms part of the definitional chain for crypto-assets: only digital representations of value or rights that use DLT or similar technology fall within MiCA's scope. Non-DLT digital assets such as conventional digital vouchers, loyalty points on centralised databases, or digital representations of existing financial instruments may not qualify as crypto-assets under MiCA.

title: Orderly Wind-Down of ART Issuers
content: Article 46 of MiCA requires issuers of asset-referenced tokens to have in place a plan for the orderly wind-down of their activities, including the continuation or wind-down of the activities related to the ART that are performed by third-party entities, when the ART fails to meet the requirements of the Regulation. The wind-down plan must demonstrate the ability of the issuer to carry out the wind-down without causing undue economic harm to the holders of the ART, to the stability of the markets of the reserve assets, or to the continuity of any critical functions. The wind-down plan must be regularly updated and submitted to the competent authority. The plan must include procedures for returning reserve assets to holders, timelines for redemption, communication strategies, and the management of outstanding liabilities. For significant ART issuers, the wind-down plan must be approved by the EBA and must include more detailed stress scenarios. The requirement ensures that ART issuers are prepared for scenarios in which they must cease operations and that holders' interests are protected throughout any such process.

title: Prevention and Detection of Market Abuse
content: Article 92 of MiCA requires any person professionally arranging or executing transactions in crypto-assets admitted to trading on a trading platform to have in place effective arrangements, systems, and procedures to prevent and detect market abuse. Such persons must report orders and transactions, including any cancellation or modification thereof, that could constitute insider dealing, market manipulation, or attempted insider dealing or market manipulation, to the competent authority without delay. CASPs operating a trading platform must establish and maintain effective arrangements, systems, and procedures to monitor orders and transactions executed on the platform and to detect and identify instances of market abuse. Trading platform operators must immediately report any suspicious orders or transactions to the competent authority. These obligations apply regardless of whether the suspicious activity was detected by automated systems or by human review. The competent authority receiving a suspicious transaction report must transmit such information immediately to the competent authorities of the trading platforms concerned. These provisions establish a comprehensive market surveillance framework that adapts traditional financial market integrity concepts to the unique characteristics of crypto-asset markets.

title: Assessment of Intended Acquisitions of CASPs
content: Article 77 of MiCA establishes a procedure for the assessment of proposed acquisitions of qualifying holdings in CASPs, mirroring the qualifying holding assessment procedures applicable to regulated financial institutions. Any natural or legal person that has taken a decision to acquire, directly or indirectly, a qualifying holding in a CASP, or to further increase such a holding so that the proportion of voting rights or capital held would reach or exceed 20%, 30%, or 50%, or so that the CASP would become a subsidiary, must notify the competent authority in writing prior to the acquisition. The competent authority has 60 working days to assess the proposed acquisition and may oppose it if there are reasonable grounds for doing so based on criteria including the reputation of the proposed acquirer, the financial soundness of the proposed acquirer, whether the CASP will be able to continue to comply with MiCA requirements, whether there are reasonable grounds to suspect money laundering or terrorist financing, and the reputation and experience of any person who will direct the business. This provision prevents unfit persons from acquiring control over CASPs.

title: CASP Complaint Handling Procedures
content: Article 83 of MiCA requires crypto-asset service providers to establish and maintain effective and transparent procedures for the prompt, fair, and consistent handling of complaints received from clients. CASPs must publish a description of the complaint-handling procedures on their website and make it available to clients free of charge. Clients must be able to file complaints free of charge. CASPs must investigate all complaints in a timely and fair manner and communicate the outcome of such investigations to the complainant within a reasonable time period. CASPs must establish a function responsible for investigating complaints and must ensure that personnel involved in complaint handling have the appropriate level of knowledge and experience. Competent authorities may require CASPs to provide them with information regarding complaints received, including the number of complaints, the nature of complaints, and the resolution thereof. ESMA shall develop regulatory technical standards specifying the requirements, templates, and procedures for complaint handling. These provisions ensure that retail clients have accessible mechanisms for addressing grievances and that CASPs have internal processes for identifying systemic issues.

title: ESMA Register of CASPs and Crypto-Asset White Papers
content: Article 109 of MiCA requires ESMA to establish and maintain a register of all crypto-asset service providers authorised under MiCA, all crypto-asset white papers notified to competent authorities, and all issuers of asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens authorised or notified under MiCA. The register must be publicly accessible on the ESMA website and must be updated regularly. For each CASP, the register must include the name and LEI, the crypto-asset services the CASP is authorised to provide, the home Member State, and the Member States in which the CASP provides services. For white papers, the register must include the name of the crypto-asset, the name of the issuer, and the date of notification. For ART and EMT issuers, the register must include the name of the token, the name of the issuer, and the date of authorisation. The register serves as the authoritative source for confirming whether a CASP is authorised to operate in the EU and provides transparency to consumers, regulators, and market participants. Competent authorities must transmit relevant information to ESMA without undue delay for inclusion in the register.
